coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Eucommia ulmoides-derived carbon dots for anti-inflammatory and accelerated wound healing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Susu
Tong, Jiahui
Liu, Yingyu
Qin, Danlei
Yan, Jingyu
Peng, Hongyi
Sun, Lingxiang
Jing, Xuan
Wu, Xiuping
Li, Bing
coredata.dc:description: Inflammation affects the pathology of wound healing and is strongly associated with many chronic wounds that do not heal. Natural herbs with anti-inflammatory effects have received much attention in clinical treatment because they are inexpensive, readily available, safe, and effective. In this study, EUO-NAC-CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method in which Eucommia ulmoides (EUO) and n-acetylcysteine (NAC) were used as carbon sources. EUO-NAC-CDs have a small particle size distribution with an average particle size of 2.84 nm, emit stable blue-green fluorescence, and are biocompatible. EUO-NAC-CDs have been used for in vitro bioimaging, where high anti-inflammatory activity and accelerated wound healing have been demonstrated in vivo and in vitro. Additionally, EUO-NAC-CDs significantly decreased the expression of TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β and increased the expression of IL-10, suggesting that EUO-NAC-CDs had good anti-inflammatory effects. In a rat model of skin defects, EUO-NAC-CDs promoted wound healing, stimulated the formation of blood vessels and tissue regeneration near the wound, increased the expression of CD31, VEGF, and CD206, and decreased the expression of INOS, further demonstrating the therapeutic function of CDs. Therefore, fluorescent EUO-NAC-CDs can be effective in clinical wound treatment as imaging tools and functional wound dressings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Eucommia ulmoides Oliv.
Anti-inflammation
Wound healing
Bioimaging

FL imaging of CDs in vitro
Cellular uptake by HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (82470959), Science and Technology Innovation Leader and Key Talent Team Project of Shanxi Province (202204051002034), Key Research and Development Plan of Shanxi Province (202102130501002), Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2024-076\u30012022-120), Key national science and technology cooperation project of Shanxi Provincial Department of Science and Technology (202204041101004) and Four\u201CBatches\u201D Innovation Project of Invigorating Medical through Science and Technology of Shanxi Province\uFF082023XM013\uFF09, Scientific Activities of Selected Returned Overseas Professionals in Shanxi Province\uFF0820240016\uFF09, Scientific research project of Shanxi Traditional Chinese Medicine Administration(2024ZYYB050), Shanxi Provincial Basic Research Program (Free Exploration) (20210302124275).

This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 82470959 ), Science and Technology Innovation Leader and Key Talent Team Project of Shanxi Province ( 202204051002034 ), Key Research and Development Plan of Shanxi Province ( 202102130501002 ), Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China ( 2024-076\u3001 2022-120 ), Key national science and technology cooperation project of Shanxi Provincial Department of Science and Technology ( 202204041101004 ) and Four\u201CBatches\u201D Innovation Project of Invigorating Medical through Science and Technology of Shanxi Province \uFF082023XM013 \uFF09, Scientific Activities of Selected Returned Overseas Professionals in Shanxi Province \uFF0820240016 \uFF09, Scientific research project of Shanxi Traditional Chinese Medicine Administration (2024ZYYB050 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and size distribution histogram of the CDs. (b) The images of CDs were obtained by HRTEM. (c) XRD spectrum of the CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of the CDs. (e) Full XPS spectrum of the CDs. The XPS spectra of (f) C, (g) O, and (h) N in the CDs. (i) Zeta potential of the CDs. (j) Both 365 nm and natural light were used to take pictures of the CD solution. The absorption, excitation, and emission wavelengths were measured using UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometers. (k) CD emission spectra at various wavelengths of stimulation. (l) Fluorescence emission wavelengths of the CDs at different pH values.

(a) After coculturing HUVECs and CDs in vitro for one, three, and five days, the viability of the CCK-8 cells was assessed. (b) The viability of CCK-8 cells was measured after one, three, and five days of coculture of RAW264.7 cells with CDs in vitro. (c) CDs were used at different concentrations in hemolysis tests. (d) HUVECs were stained with calcein AM dye (green) on days 1, 3, and 5. (e) Scratch analysis of HUVECs was performed after 24 h and 48 h of culture with or without CD excitation under optical microscopy. (f) and (g) The percentages of the cell migration area at 24 and 48 h, respectively, in the scratch assay; *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Crystal violet staining of HUVECs in different groups was performed using a Transwell assay with or without CD stimulation. (b) and (c) The numbers of migrating cells after 24 h and 48 h in the Transwell migration assays. (d) Through confocal laser imaging, the uptake of CDs by HUVECs was tracked for 24 h. (e) The uptake of CDs in RAW264.7 cells for 24 h was observed via confocal laser microscopy; *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, and ****p < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Expression of VEGF in HUVECs. (b) Expression of CD31 in HUVECs. (c) Quantification of intracellular ROS levels in HUVECs, as measured by the fluorescence intensity of DCFH-DA. (d) Quantification of ROS levels in RAW264.7 cells as measured by the fluorescence intensity of DCFH-DA. (e) Fluorescence staining of RAW264.7 cells with DAPI and phalloidin. The concentration of LPS used was 1 μg/mL.

(a) Expression of INOS in RAW264.7 cells. (b) Expression of Arg-1 in RAW264.7 cells. The levels of (c) IL-1β, (d) IL-10, and (e) TNF-α in the supernatant of RAW264.7 cells after various treatments. Relative expression level of (f) IL-6, (g) IL-10, and (h) TNF-α. The concentration of LPS was 1 μg/mL; *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, and ****p < 0.0001.

(a) Representative digital photographs of wounds on days 1, 3, 5, 7, 11, and 14. (b) Analysis of the average wound area of the control group, EUO group, and CD group on days 1, 3, 5, 7, 11, and 14. (c) The organs of SD rats were stained with H&E after intravenous injection of different solutions.

(a) H&E-stained sections of skin defects from various treatment groups. (b) Skin defect sections stained with Masson’s trichrome in various treatment groups. (c) INOS immunohistochemical staining of a rat skin abnormality. (d) CD206 immunohistochemical staining of a rat skin lesion. (e) CD31 immunohistochemical staining of a rat skin abnormality. (f) Immunohistochemical staining of VEGF in a rat skin defect. Quantitative analysis of (g) INOS, (h) CD206, (i) CD31, and (j) VEGF; *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, and ****p < 0.0001.
The primer sequences used in this study.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of Eucommia ulmoides-derived carbon dots for anti-inflammatory and accelerated wound healing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Medical University School and Hospital of Stomatology, Taiyuan 030001, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Medical University School and Hospital of Stomatology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Medical University School and Hospital of Stomatology, Taiyuan 030001, Shanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, Shanxi, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, Shanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases Prevention and New Materials, Taiyuan 030001, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases Prevention and New Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases Prevention and New Materials, Taiyuan 030001, Shanxi, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inflammation affects the pathology of wound healing and is strongly associated with many chronic wounds that do not heal. Natural herbs with anti-inflammatory effects have received much attention in clinical treatment because they are inexpensive, readily available, safe, and effective. In this study, EUO-NAC-CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method in which Eucommia ulmoides (EUO) and n-acetylcysteine (NAC) were used as carbon sources. EUO-NAC-CDs have a small particle size distribution with an average particle size of 2.84 nm, emit stable blue-green fluorescence, and are biocompatible. EUO-NAC-CDs have been used for in vitro bioimaging, where high anti-inflammatory activity and accelerated wound healing have been demonstrated in vivo and in vitro. Additionally, EUO-NAC-CDs significantly decreased the expression of TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β and increased the expression of IL-10, suggesting that EUO-NAC-CDs had good anti-inflammatory effects. In a rat model of skin defects, EUO-NAC-CDs promoted wound healing, stimulated the formation of blood vessels and tissue regeneration near the wound, increased the expression of CD31, VEGF, and CD206, and decreased the expression of INOS, further demonstrating the therapeutic function of CDs. Therefore, fluorescent EUO-NAC-CDs can be effective in clinical wound treatment as imaging tools and functional wound dressings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The skin is the largest organ in the human body. It can keep the inside environment stable and prevent the entry of harmful germs and physical, mechanical, and chemical intrusions. When the integrity of the skin is compromised, immune function can be disrupted, resulting in severe bleeding, scarring, bacterial invasion, and even death [1]. Wound healing includes hemostasis, a reduction in inflammation, the promotion of tissue cell proliferation, and tissue remodeling, which are [2] continuous and overlapping processes and highly correlated with the response of inflammatory cells and the various growth factors and cytokines released by the wound. Some chronic wounds, such as those caused by diabetes and infections, have a long healing period [3] and are always in a self-sustaining inflammation phase, which makes it difficult to achieve an orderly healing phase [4,5]. Inhibiting hyperinflation accelerates the regression of the inflammatory phase and facilitates the formation of the proliferative and remodeling phases. However, traditional dressings have poor anti-inflammatory properties, the skin tends to stick to materials and crusts, and challenges occur regarding biocompatibility and metabolic properties. Therefore, an effective material is needed to inhibit inflammation and promote wound healing. As nanotechnology continues to advance, carbon-based nanomaterials have shown great potential for important applications in hemostasis, inflammation, and wound healing.

Microspheres that are about 10 nm in diameter are called carbon dots or CDs. The high biocompatibility, fluorescent properties, low toxicity, and ease of chemical modification of these materials have recently received considerable attention [6–8]. They are widely used in biomedical fields, including biological imaging [9,10], cancer treatment [11,12], wound healing [13], protein and biomarker detection [14,15], drug delivery [16], pH detection [17], and disease detection [18]. CDs have become a popular research topic in the domains mentioned above and are considered to be suitable substitutes for semiconductor quantum dots [19]. With an increase in environmental awareness among the masses, the idea of “green” chemistry as a production method has become popular, and the use of “green” biomass as a raw material has gained popularity in production research. Various green carbon precursors have been studied and applied, including Chinese herbal medicine [20], solid biomass [21], ink [22], and some pharmaceuticals [23–25], to achieve economical and eco-friendly material synthesis. Among these green precursors, Chinese herbal medicine has received much attention because of its high yield, low cost, easy availability, safety, and efficacy.

Eucommia ulmoides Oliv. (EUO) is a traditional Chinese herbal medicine. Various active constituents of EUO, including iridoid terpenoids, lignans, flavonoids, phenylpropanoids, and polysaccharides, have been identified [26]. The active ingredient in Eucommia has antihypertensive, anti-inflammatory, and antitumor effects[27]. It is rich in carbon and is an excellent green ingredient. Le et al.[28] used different drying methods to dry male flowers of EUO and systematically evaluated the nutritional value of these flowers using related methods. Xue et al. [29]used Eucommia branches to make different types of refined wood vinegar to extend the shelf-life of typical berries after harvest. Su et al. [30]used Eucommia as a raw material to prepare activated carbon for supercapacitors; their study served as a valuable reference for the high-value utilization and application prospects of Eucommia. Based on these studies, we synthesized CDs with multibioactive Eucommia, aiming to achieve or even exceed the effectiveness of conventional dressings and thus promote wound healing. For many years, N-acetylcysteine (NAC) has been used as a precursor to glutathione and cysteine in several illnesses [31]. When NAC was discovered, it was thought to be a mucolytic drug with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant qualities that protects lung tissue and effectively treats chronic obstructive pulmonary disease [32]. Under pathological circumstances, NAC acts as a ROS scavenger, decreasing oxidative stress and inflammation. Moreover, an appropriate amount of N can increase the yield of CD powder after freeze-drying [33]. Therefore, in this study, NAC was added during the preparation of CDs to improve the anti-inflammatory effect and accelerate CD production.

Using EUO and NAC as carbon sources, new CDs denoted as EUO-NAC-CDs were prepared based on a hydrothermal method. The morphology, crystal structure, and fluorescence properties of the CDs were analyzed via ultraviolet absorption spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL), surface composition, and surface functional groups. The ability of CDs to facilitate cell imaging, cell migration, angiogenesis, and inflammation in human umbilical vein endothelial cells and mouse mononuclear macrophage leukemia cells was studied in vitro, and the ability of the synthesized CDs to accelerate wound healing was subsequently verified in vivo in mouse skin defect models. This study provided a theoretical basis for the promotion of tissue regeneration by CDs in vivo and in vitro and a novel nanomaterial for clinical wound treatment.

Eucommia powder was purchased from Shanxi Sinuote Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The powder was extracted from Eucommia bark using the water extraction method. It was then sprayed and dried, and finally, 50 µm particles were obtained using a 150-mesh screen. NAC was purchased from Yisheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd., ThermoFisher Scientific (Gibco, USA), supplemented with fetal bovine serum (FBS), a penicillin–streptomycin-gentamicin solution (100 × ). A CCK-8 cell viability test kit and DAPI staining solution were obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Bestbio (Nanjing, China) provided the AM/PI cell viability staining kit, Cytoskeleton, Inc. (USA) delivered the phalloidin staining solution, and Biovake (USA) provided the 1000 kDa molecular weight dialysis bag. Mouse mononuclear macrophage leukemia cells (RAW264.7) were purchased from Tongpai Biology (Shanghai, China), while human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were purchased from Starfish Biology (Suzhou, China).

A one-step hydrothermal process was used to create fluorescent CDs with Eucommia powder as the carbon source. First, 2 g of EUO powder and 0.1 g of NAC were added to 40 mL of ultrapure water and then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined high-temperature autoclave for 10 h at 200°C. After the reaction was complete, the CD solution was naturally cooled to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous filter membrane. The managed solution was then packed into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000 kDa and soaked in a 2-L beaker containing deionized water for dialysis. The water was replaced every 24 h, and the samples were freeze-dried two days later to obtain the powder for subsequent studies. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted to assess the crystallization characteristics of the CDs, whereas transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed to examine the structure and form of the pure CDs. The chemical elements, functional groups, and compositions of the CD surface were identified via FT-IR spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). A UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorbance of the CD powder after it was dissolved in a certain quantity of deionized water. The best excitation and emission wavelengths, along with the and pH stability of CDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer after they were dissolved in deionized water and solutions at different pH values. The zeta potential test provided crucial information on the electrostatic force, solution stability, and dispersion characteristics by determining the surface charge, strength, and aggregation propensity of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the quantum yield of the relative fluorescence of CDs (QY)
ce:section.ce:para:
The standard reference for fluorescence emission is 0.56 quinine sulfate (0.1 mol/L H2SO4). The equation used to determine the fluorescence quantum yield was as follows:
(1)
ϕ x = ϕ s t ×
Ix
Ist
×
η 2 x
η 2 s t
×
Ast
Ax

Here,
ϕ x
represents the fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs,
ϕ
st represents the quantum yield of a reference fluorescence standard, I
x
and
I
st represent the fluorescent signals of the CDs and reference standard, respectively,
η
2
x
and
η
2st represent the photobleaching factors of the sample and reference standard, and Ast and Ax represent the absorbances of the reference standard and CDs, respectively.

We conducted the CCK-8 test to determine the biocompatibility of CDs and assess the effect of CDs on cell proliferation. HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells were inoculated in 96-well plates at densities of 5 × 104 and 2 × 104 cells per well, respectively. No cells were inoculated in the blank control. After 24 h of cell adhesion, the original medium was discarded, and different concentrations of CDs were added (0, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, and 1000 μg/mL).). The 96-well plates were removed from the cell incubator after one, three, and five days. The culture medium was removed, and the 96-well plateswere cleaned with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The final concentration of CCK-8 in each well was increased to 10 % by adding 100 μL of primary medium and a predetermined amount of CCK-8 reagent were added to each well. The 96-well plates were removed after a few hours, and an enzyme tagger was used to quantify the absorbance of each well at 450 nm. The cell activity was calculated as follows:
(2)
C e l l
a c t i v i t y
%
=
ODexperiment
group - O D b l a n k
g r o u p
ODcontrol
group - O D b l a n k
g r o u p
× 100 %

After counting, HUVECs in the logarithmic growth phase were incubated in 24-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well for 24 h at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. Next, 500 μL of DMEM containing CDs at different concentrations (0, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 μg/mL) was added, and the cells were cultured for one, three, or five days (with eight replicate wells per group). The original medium was removed on the corresponding days, and three PBS washes were performed on the cells in the plate. After incubation for 25 min, the cells were stained with AM/PI and rinsed with PBS, and the viability of the cells was assessed using an inverted fluorescence microscope.

First, HUVECs were inoculated in 24-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well; three such plates were established. After the cells were overgrown, a line was drawn in each plate with a marker to simulate a wound. The first plate was stained with AM live-cell dye in a cell incubator at 37 °C for 20 min and photographed. Next, 500 mL of DMEM containing different concentrations of CDs (0, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, or 500 μg/mL) was added to these two plates, and 24 and 48 h later, the mixture was stained with the AM live-cell dye for 20 min, and wound healing was monitored.

For the Transwell migration test, HUVECs (2 × 104) were inoculated in the upper cavity of a six-well Transwell plate. The membrane was placed at the bottom of the plate. Next, 1 mL of a whole growth medium, including CD solutions of different concentrations (0, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 μg/mL), was added to the lower chamber, and 1 mL of culture medium was added to the upper chamber. The cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde and stained with 1 % crystal violet. The cells that did not migrate to the top chamber were gently cleaned with a cotton swab, following the removal of chambers after 24 and 48 h. Cell migration was observed under an inverted microscope.

Fresh blood from the SD rats was drawn from the tail vein using a 1 mL syringe. The blood was drawn and carefully combined in an anticoagulant tube containing sodium citrate or EDTA. The blood cells were obtained after centrifugation at 3000 rpm/min for 15 min. CD solutions of different concentrations (31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, and 1000μg/mL) were prepared, and 1 mL of nanomaterial solution, 1 mL of PBS solution, and 1 mL of ddH2O were mixed with 20 μL of blood cells. The material was kept at room temperature, incubated for 4 h at 37 °C, and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min. A mobile phone was used to take pictures of the hemolysis event. After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected and transferred to a 96-well plate. The hemolysis rate of the material was calculated by measuring the absorbance at 542 nm as follows:
(3)
H e m o l y s i s
r a t e
%
=
ODexperiment
group - O D p b s
O D h 2 o - O D p b s
× 100 %

The Animal Ethics Committee of Shanxi Medical University approved all animal experiments (Taiyuan, China). Male SD rats (n = 15, six to eight weeks old) were selected and divided into three groups (n = 5 rats/group) to evaluate the in vivo biocompatibility of the CDs. The tail veins of the rats were injected with saline or a CD solution every two days. On day 7, after the rats died, their organs were examined under a microscope, and their hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, and kidneys were removed for dehydration, embedding, sectioning, and H&E staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL imaging of CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: After collecting HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells in the logarithmic growth phase, 1 × 105 cells were seeded in 24-well plates that contained slivers. After the cells were cultured for 24 h, the medium was removed, and DMEM with different concentrations of CDs (31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 μg/mL) was added. Then, the cells were cultured for 12 h in the dark. After the solution was discarded, the cells were washed with PBS three times. After the cells were fixed for 20 min with 10 % paraformaldehyde (500 μL), the fixative was removed, and the cells were washed three times with PBS. After incubation with phalloidin dye for 30 min, the cells were washed three times with PBS, and the liquid part was removed. The samples were incubated with DAPI for 15 min, the dye was removed, and the samples were rinsed with PBS three times. Finally, confocal microscopy was performed at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm to probe the fluorescence imaging properties of the cells.

After growing HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells till the logarithmic phase, 1 × 104 cells were added to 24-well plates with crawling tablets. Next, 500 μL of a specific concentration of the CD solution (0, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, or 500 μg/mL) was added to each well. After 24 h, the cells were washed three times with PBS, fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 30 min, and VEGF and CD31 were added to the HUVECs. INOS and Arg-1 were added to the RAW264.7 cells, which were subsequently incubated at 4°C overnight. The cells were washed again three times and incubated with phalloidin dye for 30 min., The liquid part was discarded, and the cells were washed with PBS three times. The samples were incubated with DAPI for 15 min, the dye was discarded, and the samples were washed with PBS three times. Finally, the secretion of VEGF, CD31, INOS, and Arg-1 was observed via confocal laser microscopy.

One key indicator of inflammation in the body is an excess of reactive oxygen species (ROS). We quantified ROS in cells using a DCFH-DA fluorescent sensor to determine whether CDs reduce ROS levels during in inflammation. First, 1 × 104 HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells were inoculated separately into a 24-well plate containing sliver. The culture plates were divided into a blank control, an LPS-positive control, and an LPS and CD coculture group. After adhesion, the LPS group was stimulated with lipopolysaccharide at a concentration of 1 μg/mL, the LPS + CD group was treated with lipopolysaccharide and 250 μg/mL CD solution, and the cell culture medium was removed after 8 h of culture. In the serum-free medium, DCFH-DA was diluted in serum-free medium at a ratio of 1:1000 to a final concentration of 10 μmol/L. After the diluted DCFH-DA was added to each well, the cells were incubated for 20 min at 37 °C in a cell incubator. Next, the extra fluorescent probes were removed from the cells by washing them three times in a serum-free cell medium. Finally, a confocal microscope was used at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm to observe and photograph the samples.

First, RAW264.7 cells were inoculated in 24-well plates to record morphological changes, and 1 μg/mL LPS was added to induce M1 polarization. Then, the medium was replaced with a CD solution (250 μg/mL). After 24 h, the cells were first preserved for 10 min in a 4 % paraformaldehyde solution before being labeled with DAPI and phalloidin. Finally, confocal microscopy was performed to capture images of cells to monitor changes in cell morphology.

RAW264.7 cells (2 × 104) were inoculated in 24-well plates. After the cells had attached to the wall, 1 μg/mL LPS was added, and the mixture was incubated for 24 h. After incubation with 250 μg/mL CD solution for 24 h, the supernatant was collected, and the steps described above were followed using a commercial ELISA kit. The absorbance (OD) was determined at 450 nm by conducting an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay. The concentrations of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-10 were calculated using standard curves.

The RT-qPCR method was used to detect the expression of mRNA-level correlation indicators. The RNA concentration was calculated, RNA was reverse-transcribed into cDNA, and qPCR was performed with an instrument. The primer sequences used are shown in Table 1 . The 2–ΔΔCt method was used to determine the expression of inflammatory factors at the RNA level [34].

Male SD rats (n = 15, six to eight weeks old) were divided into three groups (n = 5 rats per group) to assess the in vivo effects of CDs on skin wound healing. These 15 rats were intraperitoneally injected with ketamine (100 mg/kg body weight) to induce anesthesia and lethargy. Four circular wounds (2 cm in diameter) were subsequently made in the back of each rat and extended to the muscle layer. The procedure followed strict aseptic principles to minimize the risk of infection. The rats were divided into a blank group, an EUO group, and a CD group. All materials were disinfected, and the wounds were treated with a specific dose according to the above methods, while the blank control group remained untreated. The materials were changed 3, 5, 7, 11, and 14 days after the operation, and the wounds were photographed. Some rats were euthanized on day 7, and the rest were euthanized on day 14. Wound tissue samples were collected. Next, the samples were treated in several ways, such as soaking in 4 % paraformaldehyde, being progressively submerged in ethanol solutions with increasing concentrations (50 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, and 100 %), and finally being submerged in xylene. After immersing the samples in paraffin wax, the resulting wound tissue was cut into 5 mm slices, followed by degreasing and rehydration treatments. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson’s trichrome were used to stain the sections on days 7 and 14 to measure collagen deposition and epithelium regeneration. On day 7, the tissue samples were stained with INOS and CD206 to assess inflammation, and on day 14, the tissue samples were stained with VEGF and CD31 to assess vascular regeneration.

All data were statistically analyzed using GraphPad Prism 9.5 and expressed as the mean ± standard deviation. GraphPad Prism and Origin 2022 were used to make the final figures. All results were considered to be statistically significant at p < 0.05(*), p < 0.01(**), p < 0.001(***), and p < 0.0001(****).

Hydrothermal methods were used to synthesize EUO-NAC-CDs from EUO and NAC at a high temperature and pressure. TEM was used to investigate the morphology, grain size, and crystal structure of the CDs. The morphology of the CDs was verified by TEM analysis. The nanoparticles were evenly distributed with good dispersion and a spherical structure (Fig. 1 a and b). The particle size ranged from 1.47 nm to 4.15 nm, with an average diameter of 2.84 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) showed that the graphite layer spacing was 0.15 nm, the particle size distribution was narrow, and the particle size was uniform. The XRD pattern of the CDs is shown in Fig. 1c; the image shows a strong diffraction peak at 24.83, corresponding to the graphitic carbon (002) plane [35]. Graphitized carbon has a (002) peak angle of about 26.5°. The stronger the carbon, the more complete its crystal structure [36,37]. The crystallinity of the prepared CD-graphitized material was high.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Fig. 1d) provided detailed information about the surface function of the CDs. The peak at 3379 cm−1 was attributed to the O–H group in the CDs, whereas the peak at 2931 cm−1 was attributed to the C–H group. The tensile vibration of C = O/C = N at 1604 cm−1 indicated that hydroxyl and carboxyl groups favor dehydration and carbonization, respectively. The peaks at 1404 cm−1 and 617 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibration of C–H in the aromatic ring, and the peak at 1296 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C-O-C.

The chemical makeup, bonding behavior, and other chemical properties of the CD surfaces, including the electron energy level distribution, were investigated via XPS. The overall XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) showed that the CDs are mainly composed of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N). A high-resolution map of the C 1 s peak (Fig. 1f) showed that there were two main types of C: 286.1 eV (C-O) and 284.8 eV (C–C). A high-resolution spectrogram of O 1 s (Fig. 1g) showed that the main components of O were C = O (532.5 eV) and O–H (531.5 eV). The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1h) showed that N mainly occurred in the form of T-N (401.2 eV) and N–H (399 eV). To summarize, the findings of the FTIR and XPS investigations aligned with numerous oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of the CDs, thus offering strong evidence for their outstanding solubility in water and high biological activity. Additionally, the zeta potential of the synthesized CDs in an aqueous medium was –10.2 mV (Fig. 1i). This was explained by the presence of carboxylic and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface, which may generate many emission traps and enhance the photoluminescence (PL) properties.

The excellent fluorescence characteristics of the EUO-NAC-CDs are shown in Fig. 1j. The CDs appeared as brown liquids under natural light, but they appeared blue-green under ultraviolet light at 365 nm. The π → π*/n → π* transition of the C = C/C = O functional group on the surface of the CDs was responsible for the absorption peak observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CD solution at 246 nm. The excitation and emission wavelengths of the CDs were determined using a fluorescence spectrometer. The CD solution was excited in the wavelength range of 347–487 nm, and the corresponding emission spectrum was recorded (Fig. 1k). The optimal excitation wavelength for the CD solution was 407 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength was 493 nm. After determining the ratio of the integrated emission intensity of the CDs to that of quinine sulfate at 407 nm and measuring the absorption intensity at each most significant absorption peak in the UV–Vis spectrum, the fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs was found to be 11.2 %. These findings showed that the CDs had greater fluorescence emission efficiency, suggesting that the CDs could perform better in terms of fluorescence emission. The CDs were dissolved in solutions at different pH values, and the solution was excited at the ideal excitation wavelength to assess the stability of the CDs at various pH values[38]. As shown in Fig. 1l, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs increased significantly in acidic environments but decreased in alkaline environments. The optimal emission wavelength of the CDs was maintained regardless of the pH, which indicated that the CDs had excellent pH stability.

The biosecurity of CDs is essential for their application in therapy. Thus, we conducted the CCK-8 assay to assess the biocompatibility of CDs in various cell types. As shown in Fig. 2 a and 2b, the cellular activity of the CDs was measured in HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells. Compared to that in the blank control group (0 μg/mL), the cells in all groups showed a significant trend toward proliferation, among which the proliferation of the cells in the 250 μg/mL group was the most prominent. However, the cell activity in the 1000 μg/mL group decreased significantly. On day 3, the proliferation of the two kinds of cells was stable, and the activity of the cells in the 1000 μg/mL group decreased significantly. On day 5, the cells in all groups proliferated, with the HUVECs proliferating most significantly. The cells in the 250 μg/mL group exhibited significant proliferation, cells in the 1000 μg/ml group showed cytotoxicity. These findings suggested that CDs are biocompatible and can promote the proliferation of these cells.

Living cells emitted green fluorescence signals in response to stimulation by a 495 nm laser (Fig. 2d). On day 1, the number of living cells at different concentrations slowly increased. On day 3, the number of living cells in the 250 μg/mL concentration group was the highest, while the number of living cells in the 31.25 μg/mL concentration group was slightly lower. On day 5, the number of living cells increased in all groups except the 500 μg/mL group. These findings supported the results of the CCK-8 assay and suggested that CDs may affect cell growth.

A hemolysis test was conducted to verify that the CDs were compatible with the blood cells. No significant blood cell rupture was recorded in the CD group at different concentrations compared to that in the control group (PBS) (Fig. 2c). This finding indicated that the CDs did not significantly harm red blood cells and they maintained their integrity. The hemolysis rate of the experimental group in the supernatant absorbance test was relatively low (<5%), which suggested that the CDs are highly biocompatible with blood cells.

To examine cell migration following CD treatment, we performed a cell scratch experiment (Fig. 2e). The results showed that the 250 μg/mL CD treatment group had the best cell migration performance, and the cell migration rate improved significantly. The results of the Transwell experiment (Fig. 3 a) revealed more HUVECs near the base of the Transwell insert in the 250 μg/mL treatment group than in any other group. The results showed that the 250 μg/mL treatment affected cell migration more significantly. We next conducted a statistical analysis of the migration and scratch tests involving HUVECs. Cells treated with 250 μg/mL CDs exhibited the greatest degree of scratch healing (Fig. 2f and 2 g); the healing was considerably better than that recorded in the other concentration groups. At 24 and 48 h, the 250 μg/mL CD solution greatly enhanced cell migration, as shown in Fig. 3b and 3c. These results indicated that CD treatment at 250 μg/mL significantly promoted the migration of HUVECs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake by HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effects of the ingestion of CDs on the cell and the distribution of CDs in the cell, CDs, DAPI, and phalloidin with green, blue, and red fluorescence, respectively, were used. Confocal imaging results were obtained after 24 h for HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells at different concentrations of CDs. The morphological characterization of RAW264.7 cells and HUVECs revealed that the CDs were safe for labeling live cells and were not cytotoxic (Fig. 3d and 3e). The cells emitted bright green fluorescence at the corresponding specific excitation wavelength. Almost the entire cytoplasm was covered by the fluorescence that the CDs emitted at various concentrations, and the fluorescence intensity was constant. The CDs penetrated the nucleus more effectively at 250 μg/mL than at other concentrations. The CDs produced stronger green fluorescence, suggesting that the CDs could fluorescently label HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells and that the CDs at 250 μg/mL could significantly facilitate imaging. Based on this finding, by performing fluorescence microscopy, CDs can be used as bioimaging markers to detect fluorescence in the cytoplasm and nucleus. The cells in our images are clearly contoured, which facilitated the visualization of morphological changes in the cells via fluorescence imaging.

Immunofluorescence was used to detect the expression of VEGF and CD31 proteins in HUVECs (Fig. 4 a and 4b) and the expression of INOS and Arg-1 proteins in RAW264.7 cells (Fig. 5 a and 5b).​ HUVECs treated with 250 μg/mL CDs significantly increased the expression of VEGF and CD31 proteins.​ In RAW264.7 cells, 250 μg/mL CD treatment significantly inhibited the expression of INOS but significantly promoted the expression of Arg-1. These results showed that EUO-NAC-CDs effectively promoted angiogenesis and had anti-inflammatory effects. Cytoskeleton staining of HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells revealed that all cells in each group were well extended, indicating that the CDs were biocompatible.

We investigated the ability of HUVECs and RAW264.7 cells to clear intracellular ROS. DCFH-DA can freely cross the cell membrane and hydrolyze into DCFH, which is converted into DCF by intracellular ROS stimulation. Hence, intracellular ROS can be detected via DCF fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 4c and 4d, the level of intracellular ROS significantly increased after 20 min of DCFH-DA stimulation, whereas the level of intracellular DCF was considerably reduced by the CDs, and almost no fluorescence was found under the microscope. These results indicated that CD nanomaterials have strong ROS scavenging abilities and can absorb excess ROS in wounds, reduce oxidative stress, and promote wound healing.

Next, we examined the anti-inflammatory properties of the CDs. First, the effect of CDs on the morphology of RAW264.7 cells was investigated by fluorescence staining. As shown in Fig. 4e, the RAW264.7 cells in the CD group were fusiform-shaped, while the cells in the LPS group were predominantly fried egg-shaped. M1 macrophages are round and have many protrusions. During wound healing, M1 macrophages secrete TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, and other inflammatory factors, which induce inflammation, reduce the recruitment of angiogenic cells, and worsen the healing process.

The level of IL-1β in the CD group was significantly lower than that in the LPS group (Fig. 5c). Similarly, the introduction of CDs significantly reduced the level of TNF-α in the inflammatory cell model (Fig. 5e). CDs can also effectively promote an increase in IL-10 levels in inflammatory cell models (Fig. 5d).

The PCR results confirmed the alterations in macrophage gene expression. An inflammatory model was constructed from RAW264.7 cells induced with LPS. The mRNA levels of TNF-α (Fig. 5h) and IL-6 (Fig. 5f) increased 2.35-fold and 16.1-fold, respectively, and the mRNA level of IL-10 (Fig. 5g) decreased 4.59-fold, which proved that the inflammatory model was successfully constructed. The figure showed that the CD group had a significant decrease in TNF-α and IL-6 expression but an increase in IL-10 expression at a concentration of 250 μg/mL. The expression of proinflammatory mediators was considerably lower, and the expression of anti-inflammatory mediators was significantly greater in the model group with CDs than in the LPS group without CDs. This suggested that CDs have excellent anti-inflammatory effects on inflammatory wounds.

The results of several in vitro tests revealed that EUO-NAC-CDs are biocompatible and can stimulate cell migration and proliferation. Additionally, the results for the 250 μg/mL CD treatment group were the best; thus, we selected 250 μg/mL CDs to conduct in vivo experiments to confirm their ability to heal skin lesions. The skin defect model is a popular and extensively studied animal model for evaluating the medicinal benefits of materials [2,39]. The wound healing rate is an important measure for assessing the efficacy of treatment [40]. Digital photographs of the wound area at various intervals were taken to document any changes. As shown in Fig. 6 a and 6b, within 14 days, compared to that in the blank and EUO groups, wound healing in the 250 μg/mL CD treatment group was more pronounced and rapid. Additionally, after 14 days, the wounds in the 250 μg/mL CD group healed completely. However, no wound healing was observed in the control or EUO groups, suggesting that these CDs may promote wound healing.

Histological staining is required to identify the regeneration of damaged skin tissue. Similarly, before removing diseased slices of the rat skin, we assessed the safety of the CDs in vivo. After injecting the CD solution into the tails of the rats, we allowed them to sleep and examined the changes in various organs. No prominent difference was recorded between the experimental and control groups, as shown in Fig. 6c. These findings suggested that the CDs are biocompatible in vivo. We subsequently collected skin samples on days 7 and 14 and evaluated the degree of re-epithelization of the healed skin via H&E staining (Fig. 7 a). On day 7, skin epithelium healing was significantly incomplete in the control and EUO groups. In contrast, the epithelial cells in the CD group were fully formed. On day 14, in the CD group, the cuticle was intact, the epidermis was the thickest after regrowth, and the gap between the granulation tissues was the shortest. Masson staining was performed to obtain information on collagen deposition during skin repair. As shown in Fig. 7b, the CD groups with the densest alignment and thickest collagen bundles had better results in accelerating wound healing. Moreover, new blood vessels were abundant in the CD group, collagen fibers were well-organized, and skin appendages such as hair follicles were formed. To assess the anti-inflammatory effects of the CDs in the early stages of wound repair, we stained the skin defects of the rats via INOS and CD206 immunohistochemistry on day 7.

As shown in Fig. 7c and 7 g, the expression of INOS on the wound surface in the CD treatment group was significantly lower than that in the control and EUO groups. In contrast, the expression of CD206 in the CD treatment group was considerably greater than that in the control group and the EUO group (Fig. 7d and 7 h), which indicated that the CDs had a noticeable anti-inflammatory effect. Immunofluorescence staining of CD31 and VEGF was performed to observe angiogenesis in the late stages of wound repair. As markers of endothelial cells, CD31 and VEGF are widely used to detect new blood vessels. Therefore, we used CD31 and VEGF as measures of vascularization. On day 14, the skin of each rat was stained with the defect. As shown in Fig. 7e and 7f, the expression of CD31 and VEGF in the vascular wall of the defect in the CD group was significantly greater than that in the control group and the EUO group, which indicated that the proliferation and mobility of endothelial cells in the defect in the CD group were greater than those in the control group. The vascular healing speed was faster (Fig. 7i and 7j).

These results indicated that EUO-NAC-CDs are suitable nanomaterials for wound healing because they have satisfactory biocompatibility, faster wound healing characteristics, and significantly affect the regeneration and repair of damaged skin tissue.

We investigated EUO-NAC-CDs in other studies and found that they have good fluorescence and water solubility qualities. Compared to the raw materials of EUO, these CDs have novel properties that broaden their future application prospects. The results of our cell experiments confirmed that these CDs can enter cells for fluorescence imaging via their fluorescence properties. We verified using various methods that these compounds can promote the proliferation of multiple cell types at a particular concentration. Similarly, by conducting experiments on wound healing, inflammation, and angiogenesis, we evaluated the efficacy of CDs. We found that 250 μg/mL is the optimal concentration of CDs for stimulating tissue wound cell proliferation. By conducting immunofluorescence and immunohistochemistry examinations, we found that EUO-NAC-CDs can promote tissue vascularization, cell proliferation, and inflammation by inhibiting the expression of INOS and increasing the expression of Arg-1, CD206, VEGF, CD31, and other factors. These findings indicated that our CDs inherited some of the pharmacological effects of EUO. The CDs also exhibited novel properties, such as fluorescence and good biocompatibility. However, some aspects of EUO-specific mechanisms, such as osteogenesis, antioxidation, and the ability of CDs to promote tissue healing, were not confirmed in this study. In subsequent studies, we aim to investigate these aspects and broaden the possible uses of CDs generated from EUO and natural compounds.

To summarize, we synthesized biomass CDs using a simple and eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal method. These CDs had strong fluorescence properties, good biocompatibility, and water solubility. In vitro experiments revealed that EUO-NAC-CDs had significant anti-inflammatory activity, which increased the expression of anti-inflammatory factors (IL-10) while suppressing the overexpression of proinflammatory factors (TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β). Additionally, the results of in vivo experiments revealed that EUO-NAC-CDs have better therapeutic effects than Eucommia in a rat model of full-layer skin defects. This improvement in the effect of CDs occurred mostly because of their capacity to stimulate collagen expression, limit inflammation, enhance the development of new blood vessels, and promote connective tissue remodeling and epithelialization. This study contributes to the determination of the pharmacological effects of carbon quantum dots and provides new information for studying Chinese herbal ingredients.
Compliance with ethical standards.

With the agreement of Shanxi Medical University's Ethics Committee (approval no. KQDW-2024–001), the animal studies were carried out following the “Regulations on the Management of Laboratory Animals” and the “Guidelines for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals” issued by the Shanxi Province.

Susu Guo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Jiahui Tong: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Yingyu Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Data curation. Danlei Qin: Supervision, Methodology. Jingyu Yan: Supervision. Hongyi Peng: Validation. Lingxiang Sun: Investigation. Xuan Jing: Project administration. Xiuping Wu: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Bing Li: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (82470959),Science and Technology Innovation Leader and Key Talent Team Project of Shanxi Province (202204051002034), Key Research and Development Plan of Shanxi Province (202102130501002), Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2024-076、 2022-120), Key national science and technology cooperation project of Shanxi Provincial Department of Science and Technology (202204041101004) and Four“Batches” Innovation Project of Invigorating Medical through Science and Technology of Shanxi Province（2023XM013）,Scientific Activities of Selected Returned Overseas Professionals in Shanxi Province（20240016）,Scientific research project of Shanxi Traditional Chinese Medicine Administration(2024ZYYB050), Shanxi Provincial Basic Research Program (Free Exploration) (20210302124275).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blocking of NF-kB/p38 MAPK pathways mitigates oligodendrocyte pathology in a model of neonatal white matter injury
Acta Neurobiol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.A. Al-Griw, M. G. S., I.C. Wood, Blocking of NF-kB/p38 MAPK pathways mitigates oligodendrocyte pathology in a model of neonatal white matter injury. Acta Neurobiol 2022, 82 (1), 52–64.
